text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

text_clean: Aquí tienes un texto de 300 palabras sobre el Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan 🏺✨: --- Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan El Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan está ubicado cerca del complejo arqueológico de Chan Chan, en el distrito de Huanchaco, provincia de Trujillo, región La Libertad – Perú. Su creación tuvo como objetivo preservar, investigar y difundir la riqueza cultural del Reino Chimú, una de las civilizaciones preincaicas más importantes de la costa norte peruana. Este museo permite a los visitantes comprender mejor la historia, el arte y la organización social de la antigua ciudad de Chan Chan. El museo fue inaugurado en 2006 y cuenta con salas de exposición permanentes donde se muestran piezas arqueológicas halladas en las excavaciones del sitio. Entre los objetos más destacados se encuentran cerámicas, herramientas, utensilios domésticos, textiles, objetos de madera y metales preciosos. También se exhiben maquetas que reconstruyen la forma original de la ciudad, ayudando a los visitantes a imaginar cómo vivían los chimúes. Una de las particularidades del museo es su diseño arquitectónico, que está inspirado en las estructuras originales de Chan Chan. Sus muros de adobe evocan el estilo constructivo chimú, logrando una armonía visual con el entorno arqueológico. Además, el museo ofrece paneles informativos, mapas y material audiovisual que explican la historia de la cultura Chimú, su relación con el mar, su economía agrícola y su organización política jerarquizada. El Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan cumple un rol fundamental en la conservación del patrimonio cultural peruano. No solo protege y exhibe valiosas piezas arqueológicas, sino que también promueve la educación y la investigación científica. Es un lugar ideal para estudiantes, turistas nacionales y extranjeros que desean conocer de cerca la grandeza de esta cultura milenaria. Gracias a este museo, la historia de Chan Chan sigue viva y accesible para las futuras generaciones.
label: neutro

text_clean: El museo de sitio Chan Chan está ubicado en la misma Avenida Chan Chan, si vas desde el balneario Huanchaco, toma cualquier bus que mencione "Mall Plaza" y bajarás del bus pasando la iglesia "San José", le indicas al chofer ni bien subes que bajarás en el Museo de Sitio Chan Chan 🏛 . Ingresas y del lado izquierdo vas a ver la entrada (detrás de un muro está el ingreso). Te cuento que si ya tienes el boleto 🎟 que de ingreso porque primero fuiste al complejo arqueológico Chan Chan, presenta el boleto y la persona encargada arrancará un cuadrado que le corresponde o se identifica con el Museo. Si no tienes el boleto aún, no hay problema, te acercas a la ventanilla y pagas s/10,00 el cual te permite ingresar/visitar 4 lugares: 1) Conjunto Nik An Chan Chan 2) Huaca Arco Iris 3) Huaca La Esmerada 4) Museo de Sitio Chan Chan (también en la foto que subí puedes divisar los sitios) El museo de sitio es increíblemente Chevere asombroso, ni bien ingresas te encuentras con huacos representativos de la cultura Chimú, del lado izquierdo hay un mapa donde indica la expansión de la cultura, dónde se ubican las otras Huacas, hay reliquias, etc. Sigues el recorrido y verás una pantalla el cual te toma una foto de recuerdo, el Ministerio de Cultura del Perú 🇵🇪 lo obsequió al museo. ¡tomarte la foto es GRATIS! 🆓📸 No te cuento más sobre qué cosas hay en el museo porque no quiero arruinar la sorpresa que hay en uno de los espacios del museo :D! realmente me gustó el museo, entretenido y muy bien organizado y cuidado. La atención de los trabajadores es cálida en su recibimiento, están al tanto de lo que sucede. Recuerda ser un buen turista y cuidar el medio ambiente y el museo/completo arqueológico que visites. Demos el ejemplo.

text_clean: ¡Una visita totalmente recomendable!Fuimos un grupo de 4 personas y nos resultó mucho más cómodo y económico desplazarnos desde Trujillo en Uber. El proceso es sencillo: compras la entrada en la boletería, necesitas identificarte (DNI o Pasaporte) y luego caminas unos 200 metros hacia el ingreso. Esa caminata corta es a pleno sol, por lo que es indispensable llevar agua y sombrero, aunque afortunadamente en la entrada misma sí hay sombra para esperar. Esperamos unos 20 minutos a que se formara un grupo para entrar con un guía ( el pago es mediante aportación voluntaria). Un gran detalle es que los mayores de edad cuentan con ticket reducido. Itinerario con rampas, escalones i suelo de arena, no adaptado a sillas de rueddas. La visita dura aproximadamente 1 hora y el recorrido por este templo Moche es súper interesante. Cabe mencionar que la Huaca del Sol no es accesible, pero la Huaca de la Luna compensa todo. ¡Vale muchísimo la pena!
label: neutro

text_clean: A must see in Cusco. Pretty much the most important ceremonial Inca site in Cusco. A walking distance from the center, though it is quite a hike up. It is better to take a transport there or to come down from Tambomachay, Puka Pukara or Q'enqo and then walk down from Sacsayhuaman to the city. Don't miss any location of the archaeological site, besides the main construction with the stones up to 12 tones each, there are natural slides on the volcanic stones area and also 2 fun tunnels to go through. Explore it with time and if you wonder whether a guide is worth it, this is the place for it.

text_clean: I really enjoyed walking around these ruins. It had a great view of Cusco too. We originally were going to walk up to the ruins from Cusco but glad we didn't as it was very uphill. I wish we would've known about the local bus instead of taking a more expensive taxi that stops at Saqsaywaman and goes all the way to Tambomachay for 1 soles. Also note you cannot pay for just Saqsaywaman, you have to buy either a pass for 4 ruins for 70 soles or the big one for 150 soles. The other 3 ruins are past Saqsaywaman and in my opinion weren't really that great, but worth it if you catch the local bus for 1 soles since they're included in the 70 soles pass. We spent 2-3 hours at Saqsaywaman without a guide and the other ruins probably 2 hours with transportation so it makes for a nice day.

text_clean: Fascinating archaeological site just outside Lima! The museum is modern, clean and very well curated, offering a clear explanation of the history of Pachacamac and the different cultures that lived here — from the Lima and Wari to the Inca civilization. The ruins are vast and impressive, with temples, plazas and stunning views of the valley and the Pacific Ocean. Entry costs S/15 per person, and you can explore the site either by walking or by car. Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

text_clean: Just wow. The views are stunning and history amazing. Make sure to hire a guide. If you came up without one get one at the entrance, it's worth it. There are many details you'll miss otherwise also they happily take pictures of you or your group so no one is ever left out. Best time to go is early morning so there is less people. There are two options, walking up the stairs that can take 1-1.5hrs or a bus (20-25minutes, 12usd ticket one way). I recommend the bus. You'll probably be very tired on Machu Picchu after hiking up there, also there is no view but bushes and stairs on the way up. Either way, if you do plan to hike up then the bridge will be opened around 5am and if you take the bus, first bus leaves 5:30am and buses leave every 5 minutes, people start lining up around 3am. So plan your time well. We went in line 4:05am and got on bus 6:05. We did not save in time wise but definitely did energy wise, we ended up doing 10k steps around Machu Picchu. You can always hike down in the end. Also you can enter twice with one ticket so you can come out to use restroom and eat once and return on the mountain. ALSO down next to toilets is a little table where you can stamp your passport with Machu Picchu stamp, do it, it's beautiful.
label: neutro

text_clean: I recently had the pleasure of visiting the awe-inspiring wonder of Machu Picchu in Peru, and it is no exaggeration to say that the experience surpassed all my expectations. This ancient Incan citadel, nestled amidst the breathtaking Andean mountains, proved to be a true masterpiece of human engineering and natural beauty. The journey to Machu Picchu is an adventure in itself. I opted for the classic Inca Trail, a four-day trek that winds through rugged terrain, lush forests, and ancient ruins. The trail allowed me to immerse myself in the history and culture of the Incas, and it built up my anticipation for the grand reveal that awaited me at the end. As I approached the Sun Gate and caught my first glimpse of Machu Picchu, my breath was taken away. The sight of the citadel perched high on a ridge, surrounded by mist and verdant slopes, was truly surreal. The sheer scale and intricacy of the stone structures left me in awe, and I marveled at the ingenuity of the Incas who built this masterpiece without modern tools. Exploring Machu Picchu was like stepping back in time. The well-preserved ruins tell a story of a vibrant civilization that thrived in harmony with its natural surroundings. Walking through the Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana stone, and the Royal Tomb, I felt a deep sense of reverence for the history that unfolded within these ancient walls. The guides provided insightful explanations, bringing the site to life with captivating stories and legends. One of the highlights of my visit was ascending Huayna Picchu, the iconic peak that overlooks Machu Picchu. The climb was challenging but immensely rewarding. From the summit, I was treated to a panoramic view of the entire citadel, framed by the dramatic Andean landscape. The feeling of being on top of the world, surrounded by such beauty, was truly indescribable. The preservation efforts at Machu Picchu are commendable. The site is carefully managed to protect its integrity, and visitor numbers are regulated to maintain a sense of tranquility. This approach ensures that future generations will be able to appreciate and connect with this historical gem. Aside from the citadel itself, the surrounding area offers other attractions worth exploring. The charming town of Aguas Calientes, located at the base of Machu Picchu, offers a cozy atmosphere, delicious cuisine, and warm hospitality. After a long day of exploration, relaxing in the natural hot springs of Aguas Calientes was the perfect way to unwind. In conclusion, my visit to Machu Picchu was an extraordinary experience that will forever hold a special place in my heart. Its mystical allure, rich history, and breathtaking setting make it a must-see destination for any traveler seeking a truly transformative journey. Whether you choose to trek the Inca Trail or explore other access routes, Machu Picchu promises an adventure of a lifetime, leaving you with memories to treasure for years to come.

text_clean: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is the only continuously occupied Incan town, it is very beautiful with cobblestoned streets and adobe buildings and streams neatly directed through it by Incan stonework and a nice market of highland wares for your perusal. Visible and drawing your attention on the surrounding hillsides are a number of huge granaries/storehouses where the Incas stored the agricultural surplus. Ollantaytambo sits in the picturesque sacred valley on the course of the Urubamba River and is guarded on the abutting hillside by the formidable ruins of Incan terraces and religious structures. These you can tour with a ticket, for which the sacred valley multisite-card also serves. This requires significant stair climbing of varying heights but is well worth the views near and far that can only be had from there. The stonework is amazing and the details up top simply cannot be fully experienced from below or from pictures. As always, I cannot emphasize enough how worthwhile it to hire a guide to expound on what you are seeing. There is so much subtle detail and context thay can easily be missed, consider how miniscule amount of money it costs to hire a guide compared to the overall cost of taking a trip to such a remote but amazing place.

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo de las tumbas reales es muy bonito, antes de ingresar deben dejar sus cosas en la parte baja ya que no permiten ingresar celulares ni cámaras para tomar fotos o videos. El guiado dentro del museo está 30 soles, si conocen algo de la cultura mochica tal vez no sea necesario el guiado, ya que las personas que hacen el guiado les hace pasar muy rápido y no les permite disfrutar de todas las cerámicas, vestimenta del señor de sipan, los lugares, su cosmovisión, los entierros, los trabajos en orfebrería, sus actividades económicas, etc. Si son varias personas podrían juntarse y pagar el guiado de 30 soles, sino vayan tranquilo y disfruten del museo. Afuera del museo, pero dentro del enrejado pueden comprar en la parte trasera souvenirs de la zona que son cómodos y pueden tomarse foto, una vez hayan ido a recoger sus cosas donde las dejaron. Saliendo de ahí puede ir al museo de Brunning, que está a 4 cuadras exactamente. O tomar mototaxi hasta la tienda de kingkones San Roque, donde encontrarán todo tipo de dulces de la mejor marca que es SAN roque (en especial los antojitos que son kingkones bañados en chocolate como el chocan) , las otras marcas no las recomiendo. O sino pueden pagar un taxi o ir a la pista y tomar carro a la playa Pimentel para relajarse. Los taxis son baratos, pero deben saber negociar.
label: positivo

text_clean: Pucllana Site Museum in Miraflores offers a fascinating glimpse into Lima’s pre-Incan history. The guided tours, included in the affordable entry fee, take you through the adobe pyramid, small museum exhibits, and a native flora and fauna area in about an hour. Its central location makes it easy to visit, and the contrast of ancient ruins amid the modern city is striking. While the site is historically significant, it’s relatively small and not as visually impressive as other Peruvian ruins. A worthwhile and educational stop if you’re nearby, but not a must-see if you’re short on time.

text_clean: A must-do if you are staying in Cusco and looking for something to explore! It’s a short and inexpensive taxi ride from the city. Be sure to bring water, sunscreen, and a hat—I got burnt even with sunscreen! You can easily spend half a day here enjoying the beautiful scenery and relaxing atmosphere. A large group of alpacas roamed around when we visited, which was a fun sight. Don't miss the rock slide at the back of the park and the cave walk-through! The slide is surprisingly fast but so much fun—even for adults! We didn’t use a guide, but there were several available at the entrance for a fee. Make sure to bring soles for payment. You can also purchase a multi-day or multi-park pass to save money if you plan to visit other sites. After visiting, you can either walk a scenic trail back to Cusco or take a taxi. Cristo Blanco is nearby and just a short hike away if you want to add another stop. Overall, this place was definitely worth visiting—peaceful with stunning views.

It was around 2.30pm when I visited, so I would not be able to take advantage of seeing any other parks today. The park staff said I could buy a more expensive 10 Day Pass. There are No Informational Signs with information other than a name as you tour the park. I visited on a Sunday, and Saqsaywaman feels more like a place you come to play in the afternoon with your family. Kids and adults were running and climbing all over the "ruins". The Park Staff didn't know where I should store the bicycle while I visited the park. The Park Staff suggested parking the bicycle in the parking lot with the cars. Don't look for the park staff to welcome you, or assist your experience of Saqsaywaman in any way. Saqsaywaman has a nice overlook of Cusco. Or you can visit the Statue of Christ or the San Cristobal Church for similar overlooks of Cusco if you do not wish to buy into the 4 Park Pass scheme.

text_clean: Saqsaywaman absolutely blew me away — the scale of this place is hard to comprehend until you’re standing right next to those massive stones. 🏔️ The precision of the Inca construction is incredible, especially knowing it was built without modern tools. The views over Cusco are stunning, and it’s a great spot to take a walk, explore, and really feel the history around you. It’s less crowded than Machu Picchu, which makes the experience more relaxed and enjoyable. I’m giving it 4 stars because there’s not a lot of information on-site unless you have a guide, and it can feel a bit underwhelming if you don’t know the history beforehand. But overall, it’s definitely worth visiting — especially if you’re already in Cusco and want something impressive without a full-day trip

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...
label: positivo

text_clean: El museo de sitio es sencillo pero claro y conciso con datos claves y diagramas informativos bien explicados. El recorrido moderadamente extenso de las diversas ruinas del Santuario se puede hacer a pie, en bicicleta o en movilidad propia. Lamentablemente algunos detalles importantes de las ruinas no se pueden ver directamente si no desde una distancia considerable. Y se entiende el acceso restringido porque es lamentable ver en algunas zonas graffitis, grabados y otros atentados hechos por personas sin escrúpulos ni cultura que han deteriorado severamente éste patrimonio histórico y cultural de la humanidad.

Go to other ruins around Cusco instead. And the last recommendation: do not be caught on trap, treat locals as they treat you. Don’t overpay, bargain when possible, don’t leave tips because you were already changed twice, bring your own food and drinks to have a lunch.

text_clean: Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. text_clean: A Timeless Sacred Experience at Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary, Peru If you’re chasing awe-inspiring scenery woven with ancient wonder, Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a must-see. This UNESCO-listed wonder blends breathtaking Andean peaks, remarkable Inca architecture, and a sense of sacred mystery that lingers long after your visit. What makes it special Iconic, panoramic setting: Nestled high in the Andes, the terraced citadel rises against dramatic skies, offering vistas that feel almost otherworldly and utterly unforgettable. Architectural ingenuity: The precision of Inca stonework, the sun temples, and the agricultural terraces showcase sophisticated engineering and aesthetic harmony with the natural landscape. Rich historical context: The sanctuary anchors a wealth of stories about Inca civilization, daily life, astronomy, and ritual practice, inviting exploration and imagination. Spiritual resonance: For many visitors, the site carries a contemplative energy—an invitation to pause, reflect, and connect with history, culture, and nature. Cultural and natural blend: The combination of rocky outcrops, cloud-draped ridges, and well-preserved ruins creates a holistic experience of humanity’s ancient ingenuity in harmony with the environment. What to expect A multi-sensory journey: The textures of stone, the scent of the highland air, and the expansive views create a memorable, immersive adventure. Guided and self-guided insights: Local guides offer fascinating context, while well-marked trails allow for independent exploration at your own pace. Photographic opportunities everywhere: Every terrace, stair, and stonework promises a picture-perfect moment against the dramatic Andean backdrop. Practical tips Plan ahead for altitude and weather: The high elevation can be challenging; hydrate, pace yourself, and dress in layers. Wear sturdy footwear: Expect uneven paths, steps, and some steep sections. Start early or late: To beat crowds and catch the golden light, consider early morning or late afternoon visits. Respect local customs and preservation rules: Stay on designated paths, do not touch ancient walls, and follow guidance to protect this sacred site. Bottom line Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a monumental, life-changing destination that blends natural beauty, architectural prowess, and cultural heritage into a once-in-a-lifetime experience. It’s a must-visit for travelers seeking inspiration, historical insight, and a powerful reminder of humanity’s enduring curiosity and resilience. Highly recommended for history buffs, hikers, photographers, and anyone who wants to connect with a sacred echo of the past.

text_clean: It's Machu Picchu, what else is there to say? Beautiful photo locations, great place to see llamas up close. (A local worker informed me they were llamas, and not Alpacas) We did not use a guide service as my wife and I like the solo travel style, but from what I saw the guides were quite knowledgeable and helpful to their tour groups. Questions we had before going there I'll try to answer; 1. What should we wear? We ended up wearing casual hiking clothes. I wore a tank top, t-shirt, into hoodie with just normal pants on the bottom with work/hiking boots. My wife wore a full winter layer setup as she gets cold easily. We both had to take our top layers off as we were in the sun and got hot. Other people looked to be wearing full blown hiking gear (maybe they were doing the Gran Caverna hike or ascending to Machu Picchu without a bus) while others wore more fashionable choices for photos. Honestly the weather will decide it for it and usually mountain climates are unpredictable! 2. Which circuit should we take? We took circuit 3 and found it very nice. Can't speak much for the other two, but from what we saw online circuit 3 had the best views. 3. How can we get a bus ticket in advance? We can't answer that, we ended up buying our ticket in the office the day before our trip at the office. The tickets are for anytime, but are linked to your passport so be sure to bring it to when you buy your ticket(s). Also when you line up for the bus, you'll be organized by your entrance ticket time into Machu Picchu. The bus ride was maybe ~20 minutes, and they send a few at a time up and down. Seems frequent enough. Final Comments There is a toilet at the top but not inside the sanctuary itself. Be sure to go before you go! It's 2 Soles to use the restroom. I found the snack bar at the top to be quite nice and affordable, which is abnormal for such a touristy place. So that was nice! Pack water! Stay hydrated!

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Negotiated a private taxi to the site from Cusco as the bus fare for 5 people isn't that much of a difference to warrant the extra time travelling. Market selling all kinds of souvenirs at the entrance to the site. Tickets should be purchased in Cusco prior to your visit. Easiest option is to buy them as part of a package at Sacsayhuaman, the fabulous Incan and Pre-Incan site in the hills above Cusco. The site is fairly compact but you should allow at least 3 hours for your visit. The site is not suitable for anyone with walking difficulties. There are marked pathways to follow and handrails to help those people who struggle with heights. The Temple of the Sun at the very top of the site, made of Red Porphory stone speaks volumes as to the age of the Pre-Incan occupation. These stones are massive and incredibly weatherproof. The fact that you can only just make out the Chakhana engravings on the surface of these stones only confirms the fact that they have been in situ for thousands of years, not centuries as the guide books will tell you. The actual Incan ruins are also very impressive. The town itself is worth an overnight stay with accommodation and plenty of places to eat. You can, as an option, catch the tourist train from Cusco to Ollantaytambo as it's on the line to Machu Picchu. Toilet facilities are available inside the site. Child friendly enough but keep an eye on them as some of the drops are sheer.

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is a destination that combines historical intrigue with a bit of a physical challenge. The ascent involves a somewhat demanding staircase with quite a few steps. While it's not exceptionally high, be prepared for the climb. The entrance to Ollantaytambo is conveniently included in the partial ticket that covers four significant sites: Ollantaytambo, Moray, Pisac, and Chinchero. These are the typical inclusions in a one-day Sacred Valley tour, the ticket is not included in the tour and it costs 79 soles. If you're a student or a minor, you'll be eligible for the general ticket, which grants access to many more sites. However, if you're traveling with a group or on a tight schedule, you might not be able to fully take advantage of it. You can find tours starting at 100 soles or opt for private tours around 180 soles. I recommend purchasing the tour once you're in Cusco, as they tend to be more affordable than booking online, where they can go for around $70, which is almost double the cost. The main difference is the quality of the food. The tour I paid for was approximately 120 soles, and while the food wasn't exceptional, it was decent. Our guide recommended staying in ollantaytambo (which is almost at the end of the tour) to facilitate an early morning departure to Machu Picchu since it's much closer to the Ollantaytambo train station. The journey takes about an hour and a half, allowing you to arrive at Machu Picchu early and return later. In my case, I had already booked my round trip to Cusco, but I think is a good advise for people who is planning on going soon. Overall, Ollantaytambo has awesome views and an amazing history behind, I’m pretty sure you’ll enjoy, you definitely need a tour guide to go here, otherwise you won’t enjoy it as much. You can also buy souvenirs here.
label: positivo

text_clean: A charming Inca ruin that’s much quite and less crowded than Machu Picchu. As one of the transitioning capital for Inca empire, there’s lookout to oversee the entire Ollantaytambo town. The spectacular imperial cut and Almohadillado architecture showed the power and intelligence the Inka people had. It’s worthy the visit and better to plan more than an hour to walk around entire site without rush. The tickets has two options - General ticket (full ticket) for 140 sol includes the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary and several other ancient ruin in the Sacred Vally such as the Moray, as well as the popular Sacsayhuamán in Cusco, which itself would cost 70 sol per the staff. Or you can go for the partial ticket for 70 sol including Ollantaytambo with several different options to access the combination of other places, which some include Moray but neither cover Sacsayhuamán. They only accept cash but can take US dollar if you run out of local sol.

text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

text_clean: I recently had the pleasure of visiting the awe-inspiring wonder of Machu Picchu in Peru, and it is no exaggeration to say that the experience surpassed all my expectations. This ancient Incan citadel, nestled amidst the breathtaking Andean mountains, proved to be a true masterpiece of human engineering and natural beauty. The journey to Machu Picchu is an adventure in itself. I opted for the classic Inca Trail, a four-day trek that winds through rugged terrain, lush forests, and ancient ruins. The trail allowed me to immerse myself in the history and culture of the Incas, and it built up my anticipation for the grand reveal that awaited me at the end. As I approached the Sun Gate and caught my first glimpse of Machu Picchu, my breath was taken away. The sight of the citadel perched high on a ridge, surrounded by mist and verdant slopes, was truly surreal. The sheer scale and intricacy of the stone structures left me in awe, and I marveled at the ingenuity of the Incas who built this masterpiece without modern tools. Exploring Machu Picchu was like stepping back in time. The well-preserved ruins tell a story of a vibrant civilization that thrived in harmony with its natural surroundings. Walking through the Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana stone, and the Royal Tomb, I felt a deep sense of reverence for the history that unfolded within these ancient walls. The guides provided insightful explanations, bringing the site to life with captivating stories and legends. One of the highlights of my visit was ascending Huayna Picchu, the iconic peak that overlooks Machu Picchu. The climb was challenging but immensely rewarding. From the summit, I was treated to a panoramic view of the entire citadel, framed by the dramatic Andean landscape. The feeling of being on top of the world, surrounded by such beauty, was truly indescribable. The preservation efforts at Machu Picchu are commendable. The site is carefully managed to protect its integrity, and visitor numbers are regulated to maintain a sense of tranquility. This approach ensures that future generations will be able to appreciate and connect with this historical gem. Aside from the citadel itself, the surrounding area offers other attractions worth exploring. The charming town of Aguas Calientes, located at the base of Machu Picchu, offers a cozy atmosphere, delicious cuisine, and warm hospitality. After a long day of exploration, relaxing in the natural hot springs of Aguas Calientes was the perfect way to unwind. In conclusion, my visit to Machu Picchu was an extraordinary experience that will forever hold a special place in my heart. Its mystical allure, rich history, and breathtaking setting make it a must-see destination for any traveler seeking a truly transformative journey. Whether you choose to trek the Inca Trail or explore other access routes, Machu Picchu promises an adventure of a lifetime, leaving you with memories to treasure for years to come.
label: positivo

text_clean: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures
label: neutro

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: neutro

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: neutro

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Cuál es la mejor temporada para visitar la Huaca del Sol y la Luna? Se puede visitar durante todo el año. Pero, se tiene que tener en cuenta que durante los meses de enero a marzo el clima en Trujillo es bastante caluroso y durante los meses de abril a diciembre es más fresco.Está ubicada al norte del Perú en el departamento de la libertad, a unos 5 km al sur de Trujillo. Se puede visitar este complejo arqueológico de lunes a domingo de 9:00 a.m. hasta las 4:00 p.m. Los tickets de entrada tienen un precio de S/. 10.00 para adultos. Qué llevar: Calzado cómodo, gorro para el sol, anteojos de sol, bloqueador solar, snack, agua y ropa cómoda y un poco de plata..😋 Es obligatorio tener un documento de vacunación con esquema completo.. En mí caso que vivo en tumbes puedo comprar paquetes online por el Tripavisor ó de lo contrario tomar un bus. Tumbes a Trujillo en Bus Oltursa es la principal empresa de transporte que cuenta con esta ruta en su itinerario, ofreciendo pasajes a precios económicos de 70 y 90 soles. Los buses salen desde el terminal., que queda en la calle principal. Con relación a los alojamientos, es muy simple, se busca por Booking que tiene muy buenas ofertas, claro está que hay que hacer las reservas con anticipación. Hay presupuestos y presupuesto todo depende como uno se organize y cuánto dinero quería gastar. SUERTE EN VIAJE!!

text_clean: It's Machu Picchu, what else is there to say? Beautiful photo locations, great place to see llamas up close. (A local worker informed me they were llamas, and not Alpacas) We did not use a guide service as my wife and I like the solo travel style, but from what I saw the guides were quite knowledgeable and helpful to their tour groups. Questions we had before going there I'll try to answer; 1. What should we wear? We ended up wearing casual hiking clothes. I wore a tank top, t-shirt, into hoodie with just normal pants on the bottom with work/hiking boots. My wife wore a full winter layer setup as she gets cold easily. We both had to take our top layers off as we were in the sun and got hot. Other people looked to be wearing full blown hiking gear (maybe they were doing the Gran Caverna hike or ascending to Machu Picchu without a bus) while others wore more fashionable choices for photos. Honestly the weather will decide it for it and usually mountain climates are unpredictable! 2. Which circuit should we take? We took circuit 3 and found it very nice. Can't speak much for the other two, but from what we saw online circuit 3 had the best views. 3. How can we get a bus ticket in advance? We can't answer that, we ended up buying our ticket in the office the day before our trip at the office. The tickets are for anytime, but are linked to your passport so be sure to bring it to when you buy your ticket(s). Also when you line up for the bus, you'll be organized by your entrance ticket time into Machu Picchu. The bus ride was maybe ~20 minutes, and they send a few at a time up and down. Seems frequent enough. Final Comments There is a toilet at the top but not inside the sanctuary itself. Be sure to go before you go! It's 2 Soles to use the restroom. I found the snack bar at the top to be quite nice and affordable, which is abnormal for such a touristy place. So that was nice! Pack water! Stay hydrated!

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is a destination that combines historical intrigue with a bit of a physical challenge. The ascent involves a somewhat demanding staircase with quite a few steps. While it's not exceptionally high, be prepared for the climb. The entrance to Ollantaytambo is conveniently included in the partial ticket that covers four significant sites: Ollantaytambo, Moray, Pisac, and Chinchero. These are the typical inclusions in a one-day Sacred Valley tour, the ticket is not included in the tour and it costs 79 soles. If you're a student or a minor, you'll be eligible for the general ticket, which grants access to many more sites. However, if you're traveling with a group or on a tight schedule, you might not be able to fully take advantage of it. You can find tours starting at 100 soles or opt for private tours around 180 soles. I recommend purchasing the tour once you're in Cusco, as they tend to be more affordable than booking online, where they can go for around $70, which is almost double the cost. The main difference is the quality of the food. The tour I paid for was approximately 120 soles, and while the food wasn't exceptional, it was decent. Our guide recommended staying in ollantaytambo (which is almost at the end of the tour) to facilitate an early morning departure to Machu Picchu since it's much closer to the Ollantaytambo train station. The journey takes about an hour and a half, allowing you to arrive at Machu Picchu early and return later. In my case, I had already booked my round trip to Cusco, but I think is a good advise for people who is planning on going soon. Overall, Ollantaytambo has awesome views and an amazing history behind, I’m pretty sure you’ll enjoy, you definitely need a tour guide to go here, otherwise you won’t enjoy it as much. You can also buy souvenirs here.
label: neutro

We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

text_clean: Aquí tienes un texto de 300 palabras sobre el Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan 🏺✨: --- Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan El Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan está ubicado cerca del complejo arqueológico de Chan Chan, en el distrito de Huanchaco, provincia de Trujillo, región La Libertad – Perú. Su creación tuvo como objetivo preservar, investigar y difundir la riqueza cultural del Reino Chimú, una de las civilizaciones preincaicas más importantes de la costa norte peruana. Este museo permite a los visitantes comprender mejor la historia, el arte y la organización social de la antigua ciudad de Chan Chan. El museo fue inaugurado en 2006 y cuenta con salas de exposición permanentes donde se muestran piezas arqueológicas halladas en las excavaciones del sitio. Entre los objetos más destacados se encuentran cerámicas, herramientas, utensilios domésticos, textiles, objetos de madera y metales preciosos. También se exhiben maquetas que reconstruyen la forma original de la ciudad, ayudando a los visitantes a imaginar cómo vivían los chimúes. Una de las particularidades del museo es su diseño arquitectónico, que está inspirado en las estructuras originales de Chan Chan. Sus muros de adobe evocan el estilo constructivo chimú, logrando una armonía visual con el entorno arqueológico. Además, el museo ofrece paneles informativos, mapas y material audiovisual que explican la historia de la cultura Chimú, su relación con el mar, su economía agrícola y su organización política jerarquizada. El Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan cumple un rol fundamental en la conservación del patrimonio cultural peruano. No solo protege y exhibe valiosas piezas arqueológicas, sino que también promueve la educación y la investigación científica. Es un lugar ideal para estudiantes, turistas nacionales y extranjeros que desean conocer de cerca la grandeza de esta cultura milenaria. Gracias a este museo, la historia de Chan Chan sigue viva y accesible para las futuras generaciones.
label: neutro

text_clean: I recently had the pleasure of visiting the awe-inspiring wonder of Machu Picchu in Peru, and it is no exaggeration to say that the experience surpassed all my expectations. This ancient Incan citadel, nestled amidst the breathtaking Andean mountains, proved to be a true masterpiece of human engineering and natural beauty. The journey to Machu Picchu is an adventure in itself. I opted for the classic Inca Trail, a four-day trek that winds through rugged terrain, lush forests, and ancient ruins. The trail allowed me to immerse myself in the history and culture of the Incas, and it built up my anticipation for the grand reveal that awaited me at the end. As I approached the Sun Gate and caught my first glimpse of Machu Picchu, my breath was taken away. The sight of the citadel perched high on a ridge, surrounded by mist and verdant slopes, was truly surreal. The sheer scale and intricacy of the stone structures left me in awe, and I marveled at the ingenuity of the Incas who built this masterpiece without modern tools. Exploring Machu Picchu was like stepping back in time. The well-preserved ruins tell a story of a vibrant civilization that thrived in harmony with its natural surroundings. Walking through the Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana stone, and the Royal Tomb, I felt a deep sense of reverence for the history that unfolded within these ancient walls. The guides provided insightful explanations, bringing the site to life with captivating stories and legends. One of the highlights of my visit was ascending Huayna Picchu, the iconic peak that overlooks Machu Picchu. The climb was challenging but immensely rewarding. From the summit, I was treated to a panoramic view of the entire citadel, framed by the dramatic Andean landscape. The feeling of being on top of the world, surrounded by such beauty, was truly indescribable. The preservation efforts at Machu Picchu are commendable. The site is carefully managed to protect its integrity, and visitor numbers are regulated to maintain a sense of tranquility. This approach ensures that future generations will be able to appreciate and connect with this historical gem. Aside from the citadel itself, the surrounding area offers other attractions worth exploring. The charming town of Aguas Calientes, located at the base of Machu Picchu, offers a cozy atmosphere, delicious cuisine, and warm hospitality. After a long day of exploration, relaxing in the natural hot springs of Aguas Calientes was the perfect way to unwind. In conclusion, my visit to Machu Picchu was an extraordinary experience that will forever hold a special place in my heart. Its mystical allure, rich history, and breathtaking setting make it a must-see destination for any traveler seeking a truly transformative journey. Whether you choose to trek the Inca Trail or explore other access routes, Machu Picchu promises an adventure of a lifetime, leaving you with memories to treasure for years to come.
label: neutro

text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: neutro

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Cuál es la mejor temporada para visitar la Huaca del Sol y la Luna? Se puede visitar durante todo el año. Pero, se tiene que tener en cuenta que durante los meses de enero a marzo el clima en Trujillo es bastante caluroso y durante los meses de abril a diciembre es más fresco.Está ubicada al norte del Perú en el departamento de la libertad, a unos 5 km al sur de Trujillo. Se puede visitar este complejo arqueológico de lunes a domingo de 9:00 a.m. hasta las 4:00 p.m. Los tickets de entrada tienen un precio de S/. 10.00 para adultos. Qué llevar: Calzado cómodo, gorro para el sol, anteojos de sol, bloqueador solar, snack, agua y ropa cómoda y un poco de plata..😋 Es obligatorio tener un documento de vacunación con esquema completo.. En mí caso que vivo en tumbes puedo comprar paquetes online por el Tripavisor ó de lo contrario tomar un bus. Tumbes a Trujillo en Bus Oltursa es la principal empresa de transporte que cuenta con esta ruta en su itinerario, ofreciendo pasajes a precios económicos de 70 y 90 soles. Los buses salen desde el terminal., que queda en la calle principal. Con relación a los alojamientos, es muy simple, se busca por Booking que tiene muy buenas ofertas, claro está que hay que hacer las reservas con anticipación. Hay presupuestos y presupuesto todo depende como uno se organize y cuánto dinero quería gastar. SUERTE EN VIAJE!!

text_clean: A Timeless Sacred Experience at Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary, Peru If you’re chasing awe-inspiring scenery woven with ancient wonder, Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a must-see. This UNESCO-listed wonder blends breathtaking Andean peaks, remarkable Inca architecture, and a sense of sacred mystery that lingers long after your visit. What makes it special Iconic, panoramic setting: Nestled high in the Andes, the terraced citadel rises against dramatic skies, offering vistas that feel almost otherworldly and utterly unforgettable. Architectural ingenuity: The precision of Inca stonework, the sun temples, and the agricultural terraces showcase sophisticated engineering and aesthetic harmony with the natural landscape. Rich historical context: The sanctuary anchors a wealth of stories about Inca civilization, daily life, astronomy, and ritual practice, inviting exploration and imagination. Spiritual resonance: For many visitors, the site carries a contemplative energy—an invitation to pause, reflect, and connect with history, culture, and nature. Cultural and natural blend: The combination of rocky outcrops, cloud-draped ridges, and well-preserved ruins creates a holistic experience of humanity’s ancient ingenuity in harmony with the environment. What to expect A multi-sensory journey: The textures of stone, the scent of the highland air, and the expansive views create a memorable, immersive adventure. Guided and self-guided insights: Local guides offer fascinating context, while well-marked trails allow for independent exploration at your own pace. Photographic opportunities everywhere: Every terrace, stair, and stonework promises a picture-perfect moment against the dramatic Andean backdrop. Practical tips Plan ahead for altitude and weather: The high elevation can be challenging; hydrate, pace yourself, and dress in layers. Wear sturdy footwear: Expect uneven paths, steps, and some steep sections. Start early or late: To beat crowds and catch the golden light, consider early morning or late afternoon visits. Respect local customs and preservation rules: Stay on designated paths, do not touch ancient walls, and follow guidance to protect this sacred site. Bottom line Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a monumental, life-changing destination that blends natural beauty, architectural prowess, and cultural heritage into a once-in-a-lifetime experience. It’s a must-visit for travelers seeking inspiration, historical insight, and a powerful reminder of humanity’s enduring curiosity and resilience. Highly recommended for history buffs, hikers, photographers, and anyone who wants to connect with a sacred echo of the past.
label: neutro

Photographic opportunities everywhere: Every terrace, stair, and stonework promises a picture-perfect moment against the dramatic Andean backdrop. Practical tips Plan ahead for altitude and weather: The high elevation can be challenging; hydrate, pace yourself, and dress in layers. Wear sturdy footwear: Expect uneven paths, steps, and some steep sections. Start early or late: To beat crowds and catch the golden light, consider early morning or late afternoon visits. Respect local customs and preservation rules: Stay on designated paths, do not touch ancient walls, and follow guidance to protect this sacred site. Bottom line Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a monumental, life-changing destination that blends natural beauty, architectural prowess, and cultural heritage into a once-in-a-lifetime experience. It’s a must-visit for travelers seeking inspiration, historical insight, and a powerful reminder of humanity’s enduring curiosity and resilience. Highly recommended for history buffs, hikers, photographers, and anyone who wants to connect with a sacred echo of the past.

text_clean: It's Machu Picchu, what else is there to say? Beautiful photo locations, great place to see llamas up close. (A local worker informed me they were llamas, and not Alpacas) We did not use a guide service as my wife and I like the solo travel style, but from what I saw the guides were quite knowledgeable and helpful to their tour groups. Questions we had before going there I'll try to answer; 1. What should we wear? We ended up wearing casual hiking clothes. I wore a tank top, t-shirt, into hoodie with just normal pants on the bottom with work/hiking boots. My wife wore a full winter layer setup as she gets cold easily. We both had to take our top layers off as we were in the sun and got hot. Other people looked to be wearing full blown hiking gear (maybe they were doing the Gran Caverna hike or ascending to Machu Picchu without a bus) while others wore more fashionable choices for photos. Honestly the weather will decide it for it and usually mountain climates are unpredictable! 2. Which circuit should we take? We took circuit 3 and found it very nice. Can't speak much for the other two, but from what we saw online circuit 3 had the best views. 3. How can we get a bus ticket in advance? We can't answer that, we ended up buying our ticket in the office the day before our trip at the office. The tickets are for anytime, but are linked to your passport so be sure to bring it to when you buy your ticket(s). Also when you line up for the bus, you'll be organized by your entrance ticket time into Machu Picchu. The bus ride was maybe ~20 minutes, and they send a few at a time up and down. Seems frequent enough. Final Comments There is a toilet at the top but not inside the sanctuary itself. Be sure to go before you go! It's 2 Soles to use the restroom. I found the snack bar at the top to be quite nice and affordable, which is abnormal for such a touristy place. So that was nice! Pack water! Stay hydrated!
label: positivo

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"
label: negativo

text_clean: I was there in the Ninethies, it was less crowded and of easier access. On the other hand, we bought entrance tickets only the day before, despite the creepy and weird official reservation system and other hold-ups tourists are being scared with. We easily found a private guide on the spot, very patient with us. If you have time, take the tourist train and try connect Cuzco/ Machupichu with Puno, e.g. Titicaca lake, over 10 hrs ride.Magical spot indeed

text_clean: Negotiated a private taxi to the site from Cusco as the bus fare for 5 people isn't that much of a difference to warrant the extra time travelling. Market selling all kinds of souvenirs at the entrance to the site. Tickets should be purchased in Cusco prior to your visit. Easiest option is to buy them as part of a package at Sacsayhuaman, the fabulous Incan and Pre-Incan site in the hills above Cusco. The site is fairly compact but you should allow at least 3 hours for your visit. The site is not suitable for anyone with walking difficulties. There are marked pathways to follow and handrails to help those people who struggle with heights. The Temple of the Sun at the very top of the site, made of Red Porphory stone speaks volumes as to the age of the Pre-Incan occupation. These stones are massive and incredibly weatherproof. The fact that you can only just make out the Chakhana engravings on the surface of these stones only confirms the fact that they have been in situ for thousands of years, not centuries as the guide books will tell you. The actual Incan ruins are also very impressive. The town itself is worth an overnight stay with accommodation and plenty of places to eat. You can, as an option, catch the tourist train from Cusco to Ollantaytambo as it's on the line to Machu Picchu. Toilet facilities are available inside the site. Child friendly enough but keep an eye on them as some of the drops are sheer.
label: neutro

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is a destination that combines historical intrigue with a bit of a physical challenge. The ascent involves a somewhat demanding staircase with quite a few steps. While it's not exceptionally high, be prepared for the climb. The entrance to Ollantaytambo is conveniently included in the partial ticket that covers four significant sites: Ollantaytambo, Moray, Pisac, and Chinchero. These are the typical inclusions in a one-day Sacred Valley tour, the ticket is not included in the tour and it costs 79 soles. If you're a student or a minor, you'll be eligible for the general ticket, which grants access to many more sites. However, if you're traveling with a group or on a tight schedule, you might not be able to fully take advantage of it. You can find tours starting at 100 soles or opt for private tours around 180 soles. I recommend purchasing the tour once you're in Cusco, as they tend to be more affordable than booking online, where they can go for around $70, which is almost double the cost. The main difference is the quality of the food. The tour I paid for was approximately 120 soles, and while the food wasn't exceptional, it was decent. Our guide recommended staying in ollantaytambo (which is almost at the end of the tour) to facilitate an early morning departure to Machu Picchu since it's much closer to the Ollantaytambo train station. The journey takes about an hour and a half, allowing you to arrive at Machu Picchu early and return later. In my case, I had already booked my round trip to Cusco, but I think is a good advise for people who is planning on going soon. Overall, Ollantaytambo has awesome views and an amazing history behind, I’m pretty sure you’ll enjoy, you definitely need a tour guide to go here, otherwise you won’t enjoy it as much. You can also buy souvenirs here.
label: positivo

text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?
label: positivo
